name: Nigerian Arabic (Shuwa)

ancestors.notes: Nigerian (Shuwa) Arabic is the western end of the same West Sudanic / Lake Chad chain as Chadian Arabic and inherits its Bedouin base; it differs mainly in completing the interdental merger and in intenser contact with Hausa/Kanuri.

allophone_rules.notes: DISABLED for Nigerian (Shuwa) Arabic — the diphthong-retaining Bedouin endpoint. ar-TD models the urban N'Djamena /aw/→[oː] monophthongization as its mainline, but Owens (1993) documents Nigerian Arabic as RETAINING /aj aw/ (jawm 'day', dawr; the ar-NG gold keeps them). The rule is inherited by id from ar-TD, so it is overridden here to an empty-phoneme no-op (the documented mechanism for a child dropping a parent's process). The word-initial ⟨وَيْ⟩/⟨يَوْ⟩ graphemes are likewise overridden in this spec's graphemes back to the diphthong readings [waj]/[jaw].

notes: Nigerian Arabic, locally Shuwa Arabic (Kanuri shuwa 'Arab nomad'; ISO shu). Spoken in Borno and Yobe states (NE Nigeria), with continua into Cameroon and Niger. It is the western tip of the West Sudanic / Lake Chad Arabic chain and the variety described by Owens (1993) A Grammar of Nigerian Arabic, the fullest grammar of the whole chain. DELTAS FROM CHADIAN ARABIC (ar-TD): (1) INTERDENTALS fully merge to stops -- ث -> /t/, ذ -> /d/, ظ -> /dˤ/ (Owens 2006 p.25: 'Nigerian Arabic has lost the interdentals'; they fall together with the plain stops rather than being variably retained as in the more conservative Chad varieties). (2) qaf ق -> /ɡ/ in the vernacular (gal 'he said'), with /q/ recovered in learned/Classical borrowings and more educated speech -- so ق is [ɡ, q]. INHERITED FROM ar-TD: jim ج -> /dʒ/ (~[ɟ]); the extended emphatic inventory /tˤ dˤ sˤ (ðˤ)/ plus phonemic /lˤ rˤ mˤ/ with minimal pairs gallab/galˤlˤab, karra/karˤrˤa, amm/amˤmˤ (Owens 2006 p.25 -- NOTE: emphasis is EXTENDED, not lost; the widely repeated 'weakened emphasis' claim is not what Owens documents); the gahawa low-vowel insertion after gutturals (the AR_TD_GAHAWA_* allophone_rules inherit from ar-TD via the OVERLAY_BY_ID mechanism keyed on graphemes_base; an ar-NG override would reuse the parent rule IDs); emphatic backing of /a aː/ -> [ɑ ɑː] from the Mashriqi base. VOWELS: a five-quality system /i e a o u/ with length (Abu-Absi & Sinaud 1968; Owens 2006), the mid /e o/ largely from DIACHRONICALLY monophthongised */aj aw/ — the historical origin of the /e o/ phonemes, NOT a synchronic categorical rule. SYNCHRONICALLY Nigerian (Shuwa) Arabic RETAINS the diphthongs /aj aw/ (kayf 'how', bajt 'house', wajn 'where'; Owens 1993, the diphthong-retaining Bedouin type), so no /aj aw/→[eː oː] monophthongization surfaces. Since the ar-TD parent now models the urban N'Djamena /aw/→[oː] merger (rule TD_MONO_AW) plus the word-initial ⟨وَيْ⟩/⟨يَوْ⟩ monophthong graphemes, ar-NG explicitly OVERRIDES them back to retention: TD_MONO_AW is redeclared with empty phonemes (a no-op) and ⟨وَيْ⟩/⟨يَوْ⟩ are remapped to [waj]/[jaw]. The ar-NG gold deliberately keeps these diphthongs. SUBSTRATE (phonology, not lexicon): implosive allophones [ɓ ɗ], velar nasal /ŋ/, and the *t > [ɗˤ] emphatic implosive attributed to Fulfulde contact (Owens 2006 p.19). Incipient lexical tone is debated in the literature and is NOT encoded here (Owens 1993).

sources.author: Owens, Jonathan
sources.title: A Grammar of Nigerian Arabic
sources.notes: The primary descriptive grammar of Nigerian (Shuwa) Arabic and of the West Sudanic chain: qaf -> [ɡ] (gal), full loss of the interdentals, the gahawa low-vowel insertion, the extended emphatic inventory, and the (debated) question of incipient tone.

sources.author: Owens, Jonathan
sources.title: A Linguistic History of Arabic
sources.notes: Read in full text. p.25: Nigerian Arabic has lost the interdentals; six phonemic emphatics (the four Classical plus /lˤ rˤ mˤ/) with minimal pairs gallab/galˤlˤab, karra/karˤrˤa, amm/amˤmˤ; /a/-insertion after a guttural (ahamar < *ʔaḥmar). p.19: *t > [ɗˤ] emphatic implosive from Fulfulde contact. p.13-14: 1sg perfect -t loss with stress evidence.

sources.author: Abu-Absi, Samir & Sinaud, Andre
sources.title: Basic Chad Arabic: The Pre-Speech Phase; Comprehension Texts
sources.notes: Cited (via the Wikipedia Shuwa Arabic phonology table) for the five-vowel system /i e a o u/ with length in Nigerian/Chad Arabic.

sources.author: Kaye, Alan S.
sources.title: Chadian and Sudanese Arabic in the Light of Comparative Arabic Dialectology
sources.notes: Comparative reference for the Chadian/Sudanese group: qaf -> /ɡ/, interdental loss, monophthongisation of */aj aw/ to /eː oː/.

sources.author: Prochazka, Stephan
sources.title: The Low Vowel Insertion Rule After Gutturals (aka the Gahawa-Syndrome) in the Arabic Dialects
sources.notes: Open-access gahawa survey. p.310: Owens 1993 is the source for Lake Chad/Nigerian gahawa. p.318: Nigerian type-2 example baxar 'back' (CaGC# > CaGaC#). p.325: 14th-c. Arabicisation from Upper Egypt.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

location.notes: Borno State -- the Nigerian heartland of Shuwa Arabic, which also reaches Yobe, Cameroon and Niger. [Wikidata Q130626 P625]

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant -- a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER.
